Notes: OOP_Using_Java- Collection Framework

Collection Framework
​​​​​​​Collection framework is a library that can be used for creating various collection classes for storing and accessing elements. It consists of 
  • Interfaces
  • Classes
  • Algorithms
Collection framework hierarchy
Hierarchy of collection framework in Java is given below.

​
Important points to note
AInterfaces List, Queue and Set extend Collection interface. Map does not extend collection interface.

Collection interface extends iterable interface which supports a method that gives iterator object. This iterator object support methods like hasNext, next() which are necessary for iterating any collection.

Hence, we can access elements of List, Queue and Set in exactly the same way, as given below:
  1. Get iterator from the collection class.
  2. Using this iterator, check if the collection has next element available. It will return true till it reaches to the end of the collection. Once it reaches the end of collection, this method returns false.
  3. Get the next element using next() method.
1) Iterator<String> iter= myColl.iterator();
    
2)  while(iter.hasNext()) {
3)        String str=iter.next();
            System.out.println(str);
     }
BMap doesn't extend collection interface. But Map returns Set of keys which can be iterated as mentioned above // Get Set of keys
Set<Integer> keys= map.keySet();

1) Iterator<Integer> iter=keys.iterator();

2) while(iter.hasNext()) {
3)        Object key=iter.next();
    // Get value for this key. 
4)    Object value=map.get(key);
}
CIterator interface provides ability to traverse the collection. ListIterator interface extends Iterator interface and provides additional facilities of iterating backwards as well as iterating from specified index also. Following is the comparison between Iterator interface and ListIterator interface.

Iterator interfaceListIterator interface
Provides methods that can be used for iterating a collection only in forwards direction.Provides methods that can be used for iterating a collection in forwards as well as backward direction
Iteration can starts only from the first element of the collection We can specify the starting index for iteration.
Supported by all classes that implement Collection interfaceSupported only by the classes that implement List interface
image widget
List interface
Features of List:
  • This interface is present in java.util package: We need to import it in our code.
  • It is an ordered collection: It means that the order in which elements are added is retained.
  • Supports Index based operations: We can access any element directly by specifying its index.
  • Allows duplicate elements: It can maintain two elements with same value. 
  • Accepts null: We can add null values in the list
  • Supports Iterator as well as ListIterator:  We can iterate in forward as well as backward direction, from start as well as from a specified position. 
  • List interface is implemented by
    • ArrayList
    • LinkedList classes.
      ​​​​​​​
They can be used as below:
ArrayList / LinkedListOutput
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.Iterator;
import java.util.ListIterator;
import java.util.List;

public class ListDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        /* Create ArrayList of Strings. Note that the reference is of         interface List<String> type and object is of ArrayList<String>         type. We can even create object of LinkedList<String> */

        List<String> arrList= new ArrayList<String>();
        // List<String> arrList= new LinkedList<String>();

        // Populate data
        arrList.add("C-DAC");
        arrList.add("ACTS");
        arrList.add("Pune"); 
        arrList.add("KNOW-IT");
​​​​​​​        arrList.add("Pune"); // Duplicate added
        arrList.add(null); // Allows null element
        
        // iteration using Iterator
        System.out.println("-- Iteration using Iterator");
        Iterator<String> iterator= arrList.iterator(); 
        while(iterator.hasNext()) {
            System.out.println(iterator.next());
        }
        /* iteration using ListIterator from 3rd index in forward         direction */
        System.out.println("-- Iteration from 3rd index");
        ListIterator<String> listIterator3= arrList.listIterator(3);
        while(listIterator3.hasNext()) {
            System.out.println(listIterator3.next());
        }

         /* Now the listIterator3 is at the end. So lets do backward         iteration using listIterator3 */
         System.out.println("-- Backward iteration by ListIterator");
         while(listIterator3.hasPrevious()) {
             System.out.println(listIterator3.previous()); 
         }
         // Access element directly using its index
         System.out.println("-- Direct access using index");
         System.out.println(arrList.get(3));
    }
}		-- Iteration using Iterator
C-DAC
ACTS
Pune
KNOW-IT
Pune
null
-- Iteration from 3rd index
KNOW-IT
Pune
null
-- Backward iteration by ListIterator
null
Pune
KNOW-IT
Pune
ACTS
C-DAC
-- Direct access using index
KNOW-IT
-----------------------------------------------
Note that:
  • Order is maintained.
  • Duplicate (Pune) is allowed.
  • null value allowed
  • Supports iterator as well as ListIterator
  • Index based access allowed
Set interface
Features of Set:
  • This interface is present in java.util package: We need to import it in our code.
  • It is an un-ordered collection: It means that the order in which elements are added is NOT retained.
  • Does NOT allow duplicate elements: It can NOT maintain two elements with same value. 
  • Supports only Iterator 

Set interface is implemented by HashSet, LinkedHashSet and TreeSet classes. Following are the features of these classes.
Common features for all:
  • Inside java.util
  • No duplicates allowed
  • Support only Iterator.
  • Non-synchronized
Specific FeatureHashSetLinkedHashSetTreeSet
StorageUses a hash table for storage. Stores the elements using hashing.Extends the HashSet class.
Uses Hashtable and Linked list		Uses a tree for storage
Null value ?Allowed.Allowed.Not allowed
Insertion order ?No Insertion order. Maintains Insertion order.No Insertion order. 
Elements sorted ?NOT sorted.NOT sorted. Sorted in ascending order.
LimitationNo order, no sortingCostly in terms of memory and speed for maintaining insertion order.Allows only those generic types that are comparable.
Suitable for:Best for fast search operations.Use it ONLY if insertion order is to be maintained.Where elements must be maintained in sorted order.

They can be used as below:
1) HashSet
import java.util.*;
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

    Set<String> set= new HashSet<String>();
    set.add("KNOW-IT"); 
    set.add("Deccan"); 
    set.add("Pune");
    set.add("FC Road"); 
    set.add("Pune"); // Duplicate. Will be ignored
    set.add(null); // null values allowed

    Iterator<String> iter= set.iterator();
    while(iter.hasNext()) {
        System.out.println(iter.next());
        }
    }
}		null
FC Road
KNOW-IT
Deccan
Pune
------------------------------------
Note:
Order NOT maintained
Duplicate NOT added
null value allowed
2) LinkedHashSet
import java.util.*;
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        Set<String> set​​​​​​​= new LinkedHashSet<String>();
        set.add("KNOW-IT"); 
        set.add("Deccan"); 
        set.add("Pune");
        set.add("FC Road"); 
        set.add("Pune"); // Duplicate. Will be ignored
        set.add(null); // null values allowed
        
        Iterator<String> iter= set.iterator();
        while(iter.hasNext()) {
            System.out.println(iter.next());
        }
    }
}		KNOW-IT
Deccan
Pune
FC Road
null
------------------------------------
Note:
Order maintained
Duplicate NOT added
null value allowed
3) TreeSet
import java.util.*;
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Set<String> set​​​​​​​= new TreeSet<String>();
        set.add("KNOW-IT"); 
        set.add("Deccan"); 
        set.add("Pune");
        set.add("FC Road"); 
        set.add("Pune"); // Duplicate. Will be ignored
        // set.add(null); /* null values NOT allowed. Will throw                                                 NullPointerException */

        Iterator<String> iter= set.iterator();
        while(iter.hasNext()) {
            System.out.println(iter.next());
        }
    }
}		Deccan
FC Road
KNOW-IT
Pune
------------------------------------
Note:
Sorted order
Duplicate NOT added
null value not allowed
Map interface
Features of Map:
  • Map interface is NOT extended from Collection interface.
  • It does NOT accept independent values of any data type. All entries must of a pair of Key and Value only.
  • Every key has an associated value with it. We can access a value only by specifying its key.
  • Every map class support a method keySet() that returns a Set of keys. We can iterate on these keys and get their associated values.
  • Map interface is implemented by:
    • HashMap
    • LinkedHashMap
    • TreeMap classes.
​​​​​
Following is the comparison between them.
Common features for all:
  • Inside java.util
  • Every entry must be a key-value pair
  • Duplicate keys not allowed. Value gets overwritten in such case.
  • Support only Iterator.
Specific FeatureHashMapLinkedHashMap TreeMap
StorageUses a hash table for storage. Stores the keys using hashing.Uses Hashtable and Linked list for storing the keysUses a tree for storage of keys
Null value ?Allowed.Allowed.Not allowed
Insertion order ?No Insertion order. Maintains Insertion order.No Insertion order. 
Elements sorted ?NOT sorted.NOT sorted. Sorted in ascending order.
LimitationNo order, no sortingCostly in terms of memory and speed for maintaining insertion order.Allows only those generic types that are comparable.
Suitable for:Best for fast search operations.Use it ONLY if insertion order is to be maintained.Where elements must be maintained in sorted order.

They can be used as below:
1) HashMap
import java.util.*;
public class MapTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        Map<Integer,String> map= new HashMap<Integer, String>();
        map.put(23, "Deccan");
        map.put(15, "KNOW-IT");
        map.put(43, "FC Road");
        map.put(50, "KNOW-IT");
        map.put(46, "Pune");
        map.put(46, "India"); // Overwrites the values for this key
        
        Set<Integer> keys= map.keySet(); // Get the Set of Keys
        Integer intKey=0;
        String strValue="";

        /* Get iterator on Set of keys and iterate on all keys */
        Iterator<Integer> iterKeys=keys.iterator();

        while(iterKeys.hasNext()) {

            intKey=iterKeys.next(); // Get key
            strValue= map.get(intKey); // Get value for this key

            System.out.println(String.format("Key: %d, Value: %s",                     intKey,strValue)); // Print the Key-value pair
        }
    }
}		Key: 50, Value: KNOW-IT
Key: 23, Value: Deccan
Key: 43, Value: FC Road
Key: 46, Value: India
Key: 15, Value: KNOW-IT
------------------------------------
Note:
  • Order of keys NOT maintained
  • Duplicate values allowed (KNOW-IT)
  • Duplicate keys overwrite existing value (Key 46)

2) LinkedHashMap
import java.util.*;
public class MapTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
       
        Map<Integer,String> map= new LinkedHashMap<Integer, String>();
        map.put(23, "Deccan");
        map.put(15, "KNOW-IT");
        map.put(43, "FC Road");
        map.put(50, "KNOW-IT");
        map.put(46, "Pune");
        map.put(46, "India"); // Overwrites the values for this key
        
        Set<Integer> keys= map.keySet(); // Get the Set of Keys
        Integer intKey=0;
        String strValue="";

        /* Get iterator on Set of keys and iterate on all keys */
        Iterator<Integer> iterKeys=keys.iterator();

        while(iterKeys.hasNext()) {

            intKey=iterKeys.next(); // Get key
            strValue= map.get(intKey); // Get value for this key

            System.out.println(String.format("Key: %d, Value: %s",                     intKey,strValue)); // Print the Key-value pair
        }
    }
}		Key: 23, Value: Deccan
Key: 15, Value: KNOW-IT
Key: 43, Value: FC Road
Key: 50, Value: KNOW-IT
Key: 46, Value: India
------------------------------------
Note:
  • Order of keys maintained
  • Duplicate values allowed
  • Duplicate keys overwrite existing value
3) TreeMap
import java.util.*;
public class MapTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        
        Map<Integer,String> map= new TreeMap<Integer, String>();
        map.put(23, "Deccan");
        map.put(15, "KNOW-IT");
        map.put(43, "FC Road");
        map.put(50, "KNOW-IT");
        map.put(46, "Pune");
        map.put(46, "India"); // Overwrites the values for this key
        
        Set<Integer> keys= map.keySet(); // Get the Set of Keys
        Integer intKey=0;
        String strValue="";

        /* Get iterator on Set of keys and iterate on all keys */
        Iterator<Integer> iterKeys=keys.iterator();

        while(iterKeys.hasNext()) {

            intKey=iterKeys.next(); // Get key
            strValue= map.get(intKey); // Get value for this key

            System.out.println(String.format("Key: %d, Value: %s",                     intKey, strValue)); // Print the Key-value pair
        }
    }
}		Key: 15, Value: KNOW-IT
Key: 23, Value: Deccan
Key: 43, Value: FC Road
Key: 46, Value: India
Key: 50, Value: KNOW-IT
------------------------------------
Note:
  • Keys are sorted 
  • Duplicate values allowed
  • Duplicate keys overwrite existing value
Selecting a collection class
Follow the decision tree given below to find out the most suitable collection class for your requirements.
​​​​​​​
​
Adding objects to collections
Role of equals method
Lets add objects of user defined class Students to ArrayList
class Student {
    String strName;
    int rollNo;
    float marks;
    public Student(String str,int i, float f) {
        strName=str;
        rollNo=i;
        marks= f;
    }
    public String toString() {
        String str= String.format("%s %d %f", strName, rollNo, marks);
        return str;
    }
}		We will add objects of this class to a collections class like ArrayList 
import java.util.*;
public class ObjectTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student [] arrStudent= new    Student[] {
                new Student("Sachin",1,12.34f),
                new Student("Anil",2,45.2f),
                new Student("Virendra",3,56.34f),
                new Student("Rohit",4,98.34f),
                new Student("Tushar",5,23.67f),
                new Student("Ravi",6,87.44f)
        };
        
        List<Student> myColl= new ArrayList<Student>();
        for(Student s:arrStudent)
            myColl.add(s);

        boolean bResult= myColl.contains(arrStudent[5]);// Found
        System.out.println(String.format("%s found by reference: %b",arrStudent[5],bResult));
        
        Student objStudent= new Student("Ravi",6,87.44f);
        bResult= myColl.contains(objStudent);// Not Found
        System.out.println(String.format("%s found by value: %b",objStudent,bResult));
    }
}		Ravi 6 87.440002 found by reference: true
Ravi 6 87.440002 found by value: false
--------------------------
Note that:
When we search by reference, the object is found in the collection. But when we search the object by value, it is NOT found. This is because by default, objects are compared by reference equality
Now we will override equals to achieve value equality in class Student and run the same code.
class Student {
    ...
    ...
    public boolean equals(Object obj) {
        if (this == obj) // Check if object is checking with itself
            return true;
        if (obj == null) // Check if object is checking with null
            return false;
        if (getClass() != obj.getClass()) // Class must be same
            return false;
        Student other = (Student) obj; // Type cast and check values
        return this.rollNo==other.rollNo &&                     this.strName.equals(other.strName) && 
                    this.marks== other.marks;
    }
}		Ravi 6 87.440002 found by reference: true
Ravi 6 87.440002 found by value: true
--------------------------
Note that:
As Equals is overriden to achieve value equality, the object is found in both the cases.
 Role of hashCode method
Lets add objects of user defined class Students to HashSet
class Student {
    String strName;
    int rollNo;
    float marks;
    public Student(String str,int i, float f) {
        strName=str;
        rollNo=i;
        marks= f;
    }
    public String toString() {
        String str= String.format("%s %d %f",strName,rollNo, marks);
        return str;
    }
    public boolean equals(Object obj) {
        if (this == obj)
            return true;
        if (obj == null)
            return false;
        if (getClass() != obj.getClass())
            return false;
        Student other = (Student) obj;
        return this.rollNo==other.rollNo && this.strName.equals(other.strName) && 
                this.marks== other.marks;
    }
}		This class has equals method but not hashCode() method overriden. Lets add it to HashSet now
import java.util.*;
public class ObjectTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student [] arrStudent= new    Student[] {
                new Student("Sachin",1,12.34f),
                new Student("Anil",223,45.2f),
                new Student("Virendra",35,56.34f),
                new Student("Rohit",498,98.34f),
                new Student("Tushar",583,23.67f),
                new Student("Ravi",611,87.44f)
        };
        
        Set<Student> myColl= new HashSet<Student>();
        for(Student s:arrStudent)
            myColl.add(s);

        boolean bResult= myColl.contains(arrStudent[5]);// Found
        System.out.println(String.format("%s found by reference: %b",arrStudent[5],bResult));
        
        Student objStudent= new Student("Ravi",611,87.44f);
        bResult= myColl.contains(objStudent);// Not Found
        System.out.println(String.format("%s found by value: %b",objStudent,bResult));
    }
}		Ravi 611 87.440002 found by reference: true
Ravi 611 87.440002 found by value: false
--------------------------
Note that:
Here equals is overriden for value equality BUT hashCode is not. So inherited definition of hashCode (from Object class) is used. This method simply return the address of object. For search by reference, address is matching hence found BUT for search by value, address is not matching hence not found. 

Now we will override hashCode() and try again.
class Student {
    ...
    ...
    public int hashCode() {
        return rollNo%10;
    }
}		Ravi 611 87.440002 found by reference: true
Ravi 611 87.440002 found by value: true
--------------------------
Note that:
As hashCode is generated based on state of object, objects with same state return same hashCode and hence it is found in both cases
Comparable and Comparator
Need
Elements in a collection get compared to each other while sorting. Algorithm which do sorting operations, expect either the objects to be of Comparable type OR another comparator object to be passed to them as parameter.
Using Comparable
class Student implements Comparable<Student>  {
    ...
    ...
    @Override
    public int compareTo(Student o) {
        return Integer.compare(rollNo, o.rollNo);
    }
}		Class student now implements the Comparable interface and return 1, 0 or -1 based on comparision of rollno.
import java.util.*;
public class CompareObjectsTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student [] arrStudent= new    Student[] {
                new Student("Sachin",1,12.34f),
                new Student("Anil",223,45.2f),
                new Student("Virendra",35,56.34f),
                new Student("Rohit",498,98.34f),
                new Student("Tushar",583,23.67f),
                new Student("Ravi",611,87.44f)
        };
        
        ArrayList<Student> myColl= new ArrayList<Student>();
        for(Student s:arrStudent)
            myColl.add(s);
        // objects of collection are comparable. So no need to pass comparator explicitly
        Collections.sort(myColl);
        
        Iterator<Student> iter= myColl.iterator();
        // Iterate using iterator
        while(iter.hasNext())
            System.out.println(iter.next());
    }
}		Sachin 1 12.340000
Virendra 35 56.340000
Anil 223 45.200001
Rohit 498 98.339996
Tushar 583 23.670000
Ravi 611 87.440002
----------------
Note that:
Students are now sorted by roll number in ascending order. You can change definition of compareTo() in class student to provide other ways of sorting. For Example by name, by marks etc

Limittation of using Comparable:
1) We get only one way of sorting as there can be only one definition of compareTo in a class.
2) This can be done only of we have access to source code of the class Student. It cant be done for classes whose source code is not available to us.

In such cases, we use Comparator as follows:
Using Comparator
class RollNoComparator implements Comparator<Student> {  
    public int compare(Student s1,Student s2) {  
        return (s1.rollNo==s2.rollNo)?0: (s1.rollNo>s2.rollNo)? 1: -1;  
    }  
}
public class CompareObjectsTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student [] arrStudent= new    Student[] {
                new Student("Sachin",1,12.34f),
                new Student("Anil",223,45.2f),
                new Student("Virendra",35,56.34f),
                new Student("Rohit",498,98.34f),
                new Student("Tushar",583,23.67f),
                new Student("Ravi",611,87.44f)
        };
        
        ArrayList<Student> myColl= new ArrayList<Student>();
        for(Student s:arrStudent)
            myColl.add(s);
        
        Collections.sort(myColl,new RollNoComparator());
        
        Iterator<Student> iter= myColl.iterator();
        // Iterate using iterator
        while(iter.hasNext())
            System.out.println(iter.next());
    }
}		Sachin 1 12.340000
Virendra 35 56.340000
Anil 223 45.200001
Rohit 498 98.339996
Tushar 583 23.670000
Ravi 611 87.440002
------------
Note that:
All students are sorted by Roll No as RollNoComparator object is passed to sort method
class MarksComparator implements Comparator<Student> {  
    public int compare(Student s1,Student s2) {  
        int val= (s1.marks==s2.marks)?0: (s1.marks>s2.marks)? 1: -1;
        return val;
    }  
}
public class CompareObjectsTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student [] arrStudent= new    Student[] {
                new Student("Sachin",1,12.34f),
                new Student("Anil",223,45.2f),
                new Student("Virendra",35,56.34f),
                new Student("Rohit",498,98.34f),
                new Student("Tushar",583,23.67f),
                new Student("Ravi",611,87.44f)
        };
        
        ArrayList<Student> myColl= new ArrayList<Student>();
        for(Student s:arrStudent)
            myColl.add(s);
        
        Collections.sort(myColl,new MarksComparator());
        
        Iterator<Student> iter= myColl.iterator();
        // Iterate using iterator
        while(iter.hasNext())
            System.out.println(iter.next());
    }
}		Sachin 1 12.340000
Tushar 583 23.670000

Note that:
All students are sorted by marks as MarksComparator object is passed to sort method
